Ah the important bit: this create_dagrun creates the *full* dagrun and thus includes creating the taskinstances for this dagrun. Considering that a Dag is an ordered combination of tasks a DagRun should also be this at any given point in time. Having this in resolves issues with some occurrences of deadlocks and depend_on_past and I do consider it important for the integrity of airflow going forward and is one of the reasons of using "create_dagrun" to make sure we will be consistent in creating dag runs.

get doesn't really create a transaction. It creates an object that represents a transaction.  As you point out, they are separate.

begin creates a transaction object as well.

begin is an application's opportunity to play nice and let everyone know what's going on.

I'm not proud of the implicit transaction model. If I had it do do over, I'd do it differently. (I think I did it this way originally based on experience with Ingres and/or Oracle.)

I'll also note that implicit behavior is useful for informal usage.  In fact, I could see use for a sort of an auto-commit mode that only allowed reads, although  I'm not sure if it could be done safely (because it could lead to invalidation of objects mid-computation).

In any case, allowing an explicit mode is an important first step. (And outside the scope of this pr. :))

This feels a little weird to me.  Two `ChromaticObject`s can only compare equal if they both have `_obj` attrs (and they're equal).  As it turns out, I think only `ChromaticObject` proper and `ChromaticFourierSqrt` can trigger this comparison, since the other subclasses override with their own `__eq__`.  Since both `ChromaticObject` and `ChromaticFourierSqrt` both have `_obj` defined, this works (actually, I suppose this means the`hasattr` check isn't actually needed).  But if we ever defined another subclass without a `._obj` and without it's own `__eq__`, this might inappropriately fail.  Not sure if this really needs attention or not, but I thought I'd flag it.

I don't think semantic breaks are primarily about length... I think the idea is about breaking where phrases end, so the future changes can be focused on the actual thing you're changing. We all have pretty big screens, so more than 80 characters is nice but not going to kill anyone -- it's just the standard now.

Here's an example: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/brandon-rhodes/blog/master/texts/brandon/2012/one-sentence-per-line.rst

The code equivalent of "just fill up to 80 characters" might be....

```python
import foo; foo.method(); i = 0; if foo.method2() and foo.method3(): while i < 5: i++; print "ok";
```

Perfectly valid Python, but it's more than one "line" on a single "line"... Not saying this is an amazing analogy, but not totally useless either.

We break our code when the conceptual statement is done, so I have trouble understanding why we aim to get as close to 80 characters as possible with textual documentation. Even one-sentence-per-line would be a better than "make it as close to an 80-character-wide rectangle as possible".

Why not break it where the thought ends... (typically with a `.` or ` ,` -- you might have to think back to elementary school where we learned how to diagram sentences...).

We distinguish here between child elements and attributes of the current element. `_tag_seq` enumerates the possible child elements, in sequence, just to provide a compact way to specify successor elements for any given child tag/element.

So ZeroOrOne() is logically ZeroOrOneChildElementHavingThisTag() whereas Optional/RequiredAttribute define an attribute on this element, rather than a child element.

As far as what goes before the `del _tag_seq`, just have a look around and make it consistent with the first couple others you find that show a similar situation. I don't remember off the top of my head what convention I've adopted. I do note that elements seem to be kind of one or the other, i.e. have children or have attributes, but I expect there are a couple cases around :)

Both ZeroOrOne and Optional reflect optionality, like minOccurs="0" or use="optional". Optionality is a bit different on attributes since the most they can appear is once where a child element can be repeating.

It's a little tricky because SAML, being endlessly configurable, doesn't _require_ you to sign anything. And python-saml doesn't validate anything at initialization, only raising exceptions at runtime.

If you set the `authnRequestsSigned` setting on python-saml and you then don't provide a key/cert, python-saml will raise a runtime exception when it tries to generate the authN request. 

The approach I took here was to set `authnRequestsSigned` and related options if and only if the `SAML_SP_KEY` setting is provided to confidant. So if you provide a key, all the signing options will be set, and if you don't provide a key, no signing options will be set.

I could be convinced that Confidant's settings should more closely follow the underlying python-saml settings, but I thought it seemed bad to have multiple places where you configure the signing. (e.g. I can't think of any reason you would provide a key and _not_ want signing to be enabled.)

If you don't provide a key and don't have signing enabled, then it's typically the IDP's job to reject requests. So if you try to make a SAML AuthN request to the Google IDP, for example, it will reject unsigned requests.

A couple comments here at the design level of the config parameter.  We should *definitely* have a more descriptive name.  These user interface things are some of THE HARDEST things to change once they're live if we someday don't like it.

Also, I think it's best--for the time being--to

1. leave this configuration gate undocumented, which means removing the `alt_features` entry here, and instead adding the key to the list around line 469 (feel free to leave the description as a comment there), and
2. NOT add the command-line switch right now.  Because this is something of a beta feature right now, I don't think any of us are eager to expose it to the world, and support it indefinitely.  We still don't even know what it exactly means yet.  But as soon as we document it in `conda config --describe` and make a CLI flag for it, we're pretty much stuck with it.

So, with no CLI flag, how do we enable it for us power users here?  Just use it as an environment variable.

    CONDA_ALT_FEATURES=true conda install blarg

Or of course you can also stick it in a .condarc file somewhere.

The only worry I had about that strategy was that it delinks the localization network output from the source points and might lead people to think that the offsets would generalize to different grid configurations. For ease, I implemented it such that the control points are on a grid, but there's no reason for that to be true, and one could actually use the control point configuration as a way to express a prior about the type of transformation. (For example, if one's prior was that horizontal warps were more likely than vertical warps you could position the grid as a vertically-oriented rectangle, providing for finer horizontal warps and coarser vertical warps.)

That said, now that I think about it a little more, I don't actually think that keeping them as destination points does much to combat that misunderstanding and offsets are definitely easier to initialize.

`function.startpoint` becomes `None` in the following case:

a) CFGFast scans Function A, which is not properly terminated, and goes all the way through into Function B. If at this moment we don't know the existence of Function B at all (i.e. we never see a call to Function B, and the address of Function B is not provided to us in any form), all blocks in Function B will be considered to be part of Function A.
b) Later we see a call to Function B, but unfortunately we have scanned all blocks in Function B and generously assign them to Function A. Function B will be an empty function for now.

However Function B is only temporarily empty. It will be fixed during the post-analysis procedure where we revisit the entire graph and re-create all functions.

I remember pondering this for a while, but I'm embarrassed I didn't take complete notes on my rationale. A few python issues seem to indicate this is a long-standing debacle [1][2][3] that leaves everyone a little dissatisfied. If I understand correctly, `_GLOBAL_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` rose to prominence as a sentinel to distinguish between `None` meaning "revert to default" (old) and `None` meaning "no timeout" (present).

The python socket documentation states the default returned by getdefaulttimeout is always going to be `None`, presumably until it is set using setdefaulttimeout [4], which I think would be least useful.

So my justification boiled down to `HttpsGetter` being not much more than a fit-for-purpose `urllib2.urlopen`. I thought that the safest thing to do in my confusion would be to mimic the `urllib2.urlopen` and `OpenerDirector.open` method signatures. When I was looking for precedent to contradict my plan, I didn't see any python in the stdlib that actually uses `getdefaulttimeout`, outside of tests.

If you have a preference or think I missed the mark, just say the word.

2. https://bugs.python.org/issue4079
3. https://bugs.python.org/issue14425
4. https://docs.python.org/2/library/socket.html?highlight=socket#socket.getdefaulttimeout

Oh, and as far as _what_ it actually does. Before the tox_configure hook, Tox looks at all the environments, and creates a envconfig for each one. The envconfig is the configuration of how to run the environment, with default settings resolved. What this does is notice when we've ended up with an envconfig that Tox didn't know about to begin with, and make an envconfig for it the same way Tox would have if it had been specified in `TOXENV`.

I just now noticed this and maybe this does not have to be part of this PR, but if you are already fixing all this. Ideally, one would not format logging strings before passing them to logger, but would leave it to logger to format them. In this way you can get arguments provided outside of strings, which can help when you maybe want to translate logging strings and so on.

So the syntax would then be:

```
logger.warn("Market %s tasks as lost.", num_tasks_updated)
```

Or, I personally prefer:

```
logger.warn("Market %(num_tasks_updated)s tasks as lost.", {'num_tasks_updated': num_tasks_updated})
```

Those arguments are then available as `args` in the logging record.
